---
title: "Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design"
subtitle: "A molecular diagnostics case study inspired by the 2026 MV Hondius Andes virus cluster"
author: "Portfolio notebook"
date: "2026-05-22"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  eval: false
  echo: true
---

::: {.callout-important}
This notebook is an educational molecular diagnostics case study. It describes
assay-development reasoning, in-silico review criteria, and validation planning
at a conceptual level. It does not provide deployable primer/probe sequences,
bench protocols, reaction conditions, clinical decision rules, or instructions
for testing patient specimens.
:::

## Executive Summary

In May 2026, public health agencies reported a cluster of hantavirus disease
linked to the Dutch-flagged cruise ship MV Hondius. WHO reported that the
cluster involved Andes virus (ANDV), an American orthohantavirus associated
with hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome and unusual among hantaviruses because
limited person-to-person transmission has been documented under close-contact
conditions. CDC and ECDC both assessed broad public risk as very low while
contacts were traced, monitored, and managed through public health channels.

This portfolio notebook approaches the event as a hypothetical diagnostic
development exercise:

1. Frame the biological problem: hantavirus taxonomy, genome structure, host
   ecology, and clinical syndromes.
2. Build an epidemiological rationale for molecular detection in a travel-linked
   outbreak.
3. Design an in-silico workflow for a TaqMan-style RT-qPCR assay against Andes
   virus.
4. Define inclusivity, exclusivity, analytical-risk, and change-control
   questions an academic or industry team would need to answer before any
   laboratory validation.

The goal is to demonstrate breadth across virology, epidemiology, phylogenetics,
bioinformatics, and molecular diagnostics without presenting an operational test.

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this notebook, a reader should be able to:

- Explain why hantavirus diagnostics must account for viral diversity, host
  range, geography, and segmented RNA genome structure.
- Distinguish inclusivity from exclusivity testing in infectious disease assay
  development.
- Describe how an in-silico primer/probe design screen can reduce analytical
  risk before laboratory work.
- Build a defensible validation plan for a hypothetical TaqMan assay without
  claiming clinical performance.
- Identify key limitations of sequence-only analyses for diagnostic
  development.

## Current Event: MV Hondius Andes Virus Cluster

The 2026 outbreak provides a realistic case study because it combines a rare
pathogen, international travel, contact tracing, and rapid sequence analysis.

As of WHO's 13 May 2026 Disease Outbreak News update, 11 cases and three deaths
had been reported among people associated with MV Hondius; eight cases were
laboratory-confirmed for Andes virus, two were probable, and one was
inconclusive pending further testing. WHO assessed global population risk as low
while international contact tracing continued. ECDC's 21 May 2026 update
reported 11 total cases, including nine confirmed and two probable cases, and
stated that the EU/EEA general-population risk remained very low. CDC described
the event as a deadly Andes virus outbreak among passengers and crew of a cruise
ship in the Atlantic Ocean and stated that the risk of a pandemic and the
overall risk to the American public and travelers were extremely low.

Key points for diagnostics:

- **Outbreak setting:** a ship creates repeated close-contact opportunities and
  complicated exposure histories.
- **Etiology:** Andes virus was identified, not a generic hantavirus.
- **Transmission concern:** Andes virus is exceptional among hantaviruses
  because limited human-to-human transmission can occur in close-contact
  contexts.
- **Sequence data:** preliminary analysis of cruise-ship-associated
  Orthohantavirus andesense virus sequences was posted publicly during the
  response, illustrating how genomic epidemiology can guide assay review.
- **Public health posture:** risk to the general population remained low, but
  exposed passengers and crew required active monitoring and coordinated
  laboratory support.

## Hantavirus Biology in One Page

Hantaviruses are negative-sense RNA viruses in the order *Bunyavirales*. Human
disease is associated with rodent-borne orthohantaviruses. The International
Committee on Taxonomy of Viruses places disease-associated hantaviruses in the
family *Hantaviridae*, subfamily *Mammantavirinae*, genus *Orthohantavirus*.

Orthohantaviruses have three genome segments:

- **S segment:** encodes nucleoprotein N, the abundant structural protein that
  encapsidates viral RNA.
- **M segment:** encodes the glycoprotein precursor, cleaved into Gn and Gc,
  which mediate entry.
- **L segment:** encodes the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase.

From a diagnostic target-selection perspective:

- The **S segment** is often attractive because nucleoprotein-coding regions can
  be relatively conserved within a virus species and abundant transcripts may
  aid detection.
- The **M segment** can provide lineage information but may show greater
  diversity in glycoprotein regions under host or immune selection.
- The **L segment** contains conserved polymerase motifs that may support broad
  detection but can be less discriminating among related viruses.
- Segmented genomes create reassortment risk, so a robust assay strategy may
  use more than one target if a clinical or public-health decision depends on
  lineage certainty.

## Epidemiology and Host Ecology

Hantavirus risk is shaped by host ecology. Human infection classically follows
inhalation of aerosolized excreta or secretions from infected rodents or direct
rodent contact. Geographic patterns reflect the distribution of reservoir hosts.

Broad clinical-epidemiological groupings are:

| Group | Typical geography | Examples | Main syndrome association |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Old World orthohantaviruses | Europe and Asia | Hantaan, Dobrava, Puumala, Seoul | Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome |
| New World orthohantaviruses | Americas | Andes, Sin Nombre | Hantavirus pulmonary/cardiopulmonary syndrome |

For the MV Hondius case study, the key epidemiological feature is not that
hantaviruses suddenly became broadly transmissible. Rather, Andes virus has a
documented history of limited person-to-person transmission, and a cruise ship is
a closed travel environment where contact networks, shared cabins, caregiving,
medical evacuation, and international disembarkation can complicate response.

## Phylogeny: What the Tree Should Answer

Phylogenetics should serve an assay-development question, not just produce a
tree. For a hypothetical Andes virus TaqMan assay, the useful questions are:

1. **Target scope:** Should the assay detect all known Andes virus diversity, a
   recent outbreak lineage, or a broader American hantavirus group?
2. **Near-neighbor risk:** Which related orthohantaviruses are close enough to
   create cross-reactivity concerns?
3. **Sequence coverage:** Are available genomes representative of geography,
   time, host, and clinical cases?
4. **Segment consistency:** Do S, M, and L trees tell the same story, or is
   reassortment or incomplete sampling plausible?
5. **Outbreak stability:** Do the outbreak sequences introduce mismatches in any
   candidate binding region?

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Public sequence set] --> B[Segment-aware curation]
  B --> C[Multiple sequence alignment]
  C --> D[Phylogenetic tree per segment]
  C --> E[Conservation scan]
  D --> F[Target scope decision]
  E --> G[Candidate assay windows]
  F --> H[Inclusivity panel]
  G --> I[Primer/probe candidate ranking]
  H --> J[In-silico inclusivity]
  I --> J
  I --> K[In-silico exclusivity]
```

### Example Phylogenetic Framing

For a portfolio analysis, I would include reference sequences from:

- Andes virus lineages from Chile and Argentina, including historical human and
  reservoir-associated sequences.
- Other New World hantaviruses such as Sin Nombre, Laguna Negra, Choclo,
  Bayou, Black Creek Canal, and related taxa.
- Old World representatives such as Hantaan, Dobrava, Puumala, and Seoul as
  distant outgroups.
- Any public MV Hondius-linked sequences, tagged as outbreak sequences and
  reviewed separately from the design training set.

The tree should not be overinterpreted as transmission proof. It is mainly used
to understand diversity, confirm that target panels span known variation, and
identify near-neighbor taxa for exclusivity testing.

```python
# Illustrative only. Do not run as a diagnostic workflow.
# A real analysis would require curated accessions, versioned FASTA files,
# documented filters, and reproducible alignment/tree software.

candidate_segments = ["S", "M", "L"]

reference_panel = {
    "target": ["Andes virus sequences across geography and collection years"],
    "near_neighbors": ["other American orthohantaviruses"],
    "distant_outgroups": ["Old World orthohantaviruses"],
    "outbreak_review": ["public MV Hondius-associated sequences, if available"],
}

for segment in candidate_segments:
    print(f"Curate {segment} segment sequences")
    print("Align sequences, mask low-quality regions, build tree")
    print("Scan conserved candidate windows and annotate mismatches")
```

## Diagnostic Design Goal

The hypothetical product requirement is:

> Develop a TaqMan-style one-step RT-qPCR screening assay intended to detect
> Andes virus RNA in appropriately handled clinical or public-health specimens,
> with design evidence supporting broad inclusivity across known Andes virus
> diversity and exclusivity against near-neighbor hantaviruses, common
> respiratory pathogens, host sequences, and environmental/background organisms.

This is a design brief, not a validated intended-use statement. A real clinical
assay would require regulatory review, biosafety assessment, specimen-type
validation, analytical and clinical performance studies, controls, quality
systems, and laboratory authorization.

## Why TaqMan RT-qPCR?

A hydrolysis-probe RT-qPCR format is commonly used in molecular diagnostics
because it can combine:

- sequence-specific amplification and sequence-specific probe detection,
- real-time amplification monitoring,
- compatibility with multiplex internal controls,
- straightforward analytical validation metrics such as limit of detection,
  precision, interference, inclusivity, and cross-reactivity.

For an RNA virus, reverse transcription must precede amplification. From a
diagnostic-development perspective, the design risk is not just whether a
primer pair can amplify a reference sequence; it is whether the assay remains
robust across target diversity and does not produce misleading positives from
non-target templates.

## Target Selection Strategy

Candidate targets should be evaluated across the three segments:

| Criterion | S segment | M segment | L segment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Within-Andes conservation | Often favorable | Variable by region | Conserved motifs possible |
| Discrimination from near neighbors | Moderate to high | Often high | May be lower in conserved motifs |
| Phylogenetic information | Good for lineage context | Good for lineage/context | Good for deeper relationships |
| Diagnostic risk | Misses if local variation affects binding | Misses if highly variable | Cross-reactivity if too conserved |

Pragmatic strategy:

1. Start with conserved windows in S and L.
2. Reject windows with known outbreak-sequence mismatches near primer 3-prime
   ends or probe-critical positions.
3. Prefer windows that are conserved across target diversity but separated from
   near-neighbor hantaviruses by multiple informative differences.
4. Consider a two-target assay if single-target dropout risk is unacceptable.

## In-Silico Primer/Probe Design Constraints

The following are design-review constraints, not a complete recipe:

| Feature | Review expectation |
|---|---|
| Amplicon | Short enough for degraded RNA and efficient qPCR, while retaining specificity |
| Primers | Similar melting behavior, limited self-complementarity, limited primer-dimer risk |
| Probe | Binds within a conserved, target-informative region and avoids highly variable positions |
| 3-prime ends | Avoid target polymorphisms and avoid strong non-target complementarity |
| GC content | Avoid extremes that impair binding, amplification, or synthesis |
| Degeneracy | Use sparingly; degeneracy can improve inclusivity but reduce sensitivity and specificity |
| Multiplexing | Check interaction among all primers, probes, and internal controls |
| Sequence drift | Establish a surveillance process for new public genomes |

No candidate oligonucleotide sequences are shown here. For a portfolio piece,
the strongest demonstration is a transparent decision framework, ranked
candidate windows, and a validation plan rather than a deployable assay.

## Inclusivity Testing

**Inclusivity** asks whether target strains or target nucleic acids are expected
to test positive. In silico, the panel should cover the intended target breadth.

For an Andes virus assay, the inclusivity panel should include:

- geographically diverse Andes virus sequences,
- human and reservoir-associated sequences where available,
- historical and recent sequences,
- all available segments relevant to the chosen target,
- outbreak-associated sequences treated as a challenge set,
- partial sequences only when the candidate target region is covered and quality
  is sufficient.

In-silico inclusivity outputs should include:

- number of target sequences evaluated,
- number with full amplicon coverage,
- number with perfect primer and probe matches,
- mismatch count by oligo and position,
- special flag for mismatches near primer 3-prime ends,
- estimated dropout risk by lineage or geography,
- a list of sequences excluded because of incomplete coverage or poor quality.

```python
# Mock schema for an inclusivity review table.
# Values below are placeholders for notebook demonstration only.

import pandas as pd

inclusivity_schema = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "sequence_id": "ANDV_reference_or_lineage_id",
            "segment": "S",
            "region": "candidate_window_A",
            "forward_mismatches": "count_and_positions",
            "probe_mismatches": "count_and_positions",
            "reverse_mismatches": "count_and_positions",
            "risk_call": "pass / review / fail",
            "rationale": "e.g., no critical 3-prime mismatch",
        }
    ]
)

inclusivity_schema
```

### Inclusivity Decision Rules

An assay-development team should define decision rules before ranking
candidates. Example rules:

- **Pass:** all target sequences with adequate coverage have no critical
  primer/probe mismatches.
- **Review:** one or more target sequences have mismatches outside critical
  positions, or coverage gaps make a lineage hard to assess.
- **Fail:** a known target lineage or outbreak sequence has a mismatch pattern
  expected to reduce amplification or probe detection.

These rules should be documented and version controlled. The decision should not
be adjusted after seeing inconvenient results unless the change is justified and
recorded.

## Exclusivity Testing

**Exclusivity** asks whether non-target organisms or nucleic acids are expected
to test negative. For a molecular assay, the panel should include both
phylogenetic near neighbors and realistic sample-background organisms.

For Andes virus, exclusivity should include:

- non-Andes New World hantaviruses,
- Old World hantaviruses,
- common respiratory viruses that may be present in respiratory specimens,
- human genome and transcriptome sequences,
- rodent host sequences if environmental or reservoir samples are in scope,
- bacterial and fungal organisms common in respiratory or environmental
  backgrounds, if those matrices are claimed.

In-silico exclusivity should not rely only on short local matches. The critical
question is whether a primer pair can plausibly amplify a product of the
expected size and whether the probe can bind that product. NCBI Primer-BLAST is
useful because it combines primer design with specificity checking; NCBI advises
selecting the relevant organism and smallest appropriate database for precise
checks, or broader databases when broad coverage is needed.

```python
# Mock schema for an exclusivity screen.

exclusivity_schema = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "non_target_group": "Sin Nombre virus",
            "database": "curated_hantavirus_near_neighbor_set",
            "forward_hit": "yes/no + mismatch summary",
            "reverse_hit": "yes/no + mismatch summary",
            "probe_hit": "yes/no + mismatch summary",
            "predicted_amplicon": "none / out_of_range / plausible",
            "risk_call": "pass / review / fail",
        }
    ]
)

exclusivity_schema
```

### Cross-Reactivity Risk Matrix

| Risk tier | Example non-targets | Why they matter | Expected evidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tier 1 | Closely related American hantaviruses | Highest sequence similarity | Curated local alignment and in-silico PCR |
| Tier 2 | Old World hantaviruses | Same genus, different disease ecology | Broad BLAST or curated database search |
| Tier 3 | Human and host background | Sample matrix contamination | Human/host genome and transcriptome screen |
| Tier 4 | Respiratory co-pathogens | Clinical differential diagnosis | Panel review based on intended specimen type |
| Tier 5 | Environmental organisms | If environmental matrices are claimed | Matrix-specific database screen |

## Candidate Ranking Framework

A useful portfolio artifact is a scored design table. The values below are
placeholders; in a real project they would be computed from curated alignments
and specificity searches.

```python
candidate_scores = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "candidate": "S_window_A",
            "target_segment": "S",
            "inclusivity_score": 0.98,
            "exclusivity_score": 0.95,
            "oligo_quality_score": 0.90,
            "outbreak_sequence_score": 1.00,
            "overall_rank": 1,
            "decision": "advance to non-clinical feasibility review",
        },
        {
            "candidate": "L_window_B",
            "target_segment": "L",
            "inclusivity_score": 1.00,
            "exclusivity_score": 0.82,
            "oligo_quality_score": 0.88,
            "outbreak_sequence_score": 1.00,
            "overall_rank": 2,
            "decision": "hold as backup target; review near-neighbor risk",
        },
        {
            "candidate": "M_window_C",
            "target_segment": "M",
            "inclusivity_score": 0.86,
            "exclusivity_score": 0.97,
            "oligo_quality_score": 0.92,
            "outbreak_sequence_score": 0.90,
            "overall_rank": 3,
            "decision": "do not advance without redesign",
        },
    ]
)

candidate_scores
```

Suggested ranking components:

- **Target conservation:** fraction of target sequences without critical
  mismatches.
- **Near-neighbor separation:** absence of plausible non-target amplicons.
- **Oligo physical quality:** absence of strong self-complementarity, primer
  dimers, extreme GC, or problematic runs.
- **Outbreak robustness:** no critical mismatch in current outbreak sequences.
- **Manufacturability:** avoids unusual modifications unless justified.
- **Change-control burden:** target region is stable enough for surveillance.

## Analytical Validation Plan

After in-silico review, a real molecular diagnostic assay would need laboratory
validation. This notebook does not provide protocols, but the validation plan
would normally address:

| Performance characteristic | Question |
|---|---|
| Limit of detection | What is the lowest concentration detected with acceptable probability in each claimed specimen type? |
| Inclusivity | Does the assay detect representative target diversity? |
| Exclusivity / cross-reactivity | Does the assay avoid detection of near neighbors and likely co-occurring organisms? |
| Precision | Are results reproducible across operators, instruments, reagent lots, days, and sites? |
| Interference | Do substances or specimen matrix effects inhibit detection? |
| Stability | Are specimens, controls, and reagents stable under claimed conditions? |
| Controls | Do extraction, amplification, inhibition, positive, and negative controls behave as expected? |
| Clinical evaluation | Does assay performance support the intended use in the target population? |

CLSI MM03 describes molecular diagnostic methods for infectious diseases,
including sequence selection, performance evaluation, inhibitors,
false-positive control, interpretation, quality assurance, and regulatory
considerations. CLSI MM17 addresses validation and verification concepts for
qualitative multiplex nucleic acid assays. Those standards are useful anchors
for turning an in-silico design into a regulated diagnostic-development plan.

## Regulatory and Quality Considerations

An industry or clinical laboratory team should separate four claims:

1. **In-silico design claim:** the target window is predicted to be inclusive
   and exclusive based on the current sequence database.
2. **Analytical performance claim:** the assay detects target material under
   controlled laboratory conditions.
3. **Clinical performance claim:** the assay identifies infected patients in the
   intended population and specimen type.
4. **Public health utility claim:** the assay supports outbreak response,
   triage, surveillance, or contact investigation.

Only the first claim can be supported by this notebook. The other claims require
laboratory and clinical evidence.

Version control should capture:

- source databases and access dates,
- accession lists and exclusion criteria,
- alignment and tree software versions,
- primer/probe design software and parameters,
- candidate scoring rules,
- review sign-off,
- periodic re-analysis triggers when new sequences appear.

## Failure Modes and Mitigations

| Failure mode | How it could happen | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| False negative from sequence drift | Mutation in primer/probe binding site | Multi-sequence inclusivity screen and periodic surveillance |
| False negative from incomplete sampling | Missing lineage absent from databases | Conservative target choice and documented uncertainty |
| False positive from near neighbor | Related hantavirus amplified | Near-neighbor exclusivity panel and in-silico PCR |
| False positive from contamination | Amplicon or control contamination | Workflow controls and physical separation in real labs |
| Inhibition | Specimen matrix suppresses amplification | Internal control and specimen validation |
| Overclaiming | In-silico pass treated as clinical validation | Clear claim boundaries and regulatory review |

## Portfolio Figure Ideas

For an academic website, this notebook could be paired with:

- a map of reported case countries and travel route, using only public aggregate
  data;
- a three-panel figure showing taxonomy, genome segments, and target-selection
  logic;
- a phylogenetic tree with target and near-neighbor groups highlighted;
- a heatmap of hypothetical mismatch burden across candidate windows;
- a validation waterfall from in-silico design to analytical validation to
  clinical evaluation.

## Discussion

The MV Hondius cluster is a strong case study because it shows how modern
molecular diagnostics sits between public health and molecular biology.
Phylogeny helps define the target and near-neighbor space. Epidemiology defines
who might be tested, what specimens might be relevant, and what turnaround time
would matter. Assay design translates sequence conservation into a practical
screen, while inclusivity and exclusivity testing force the developer to state
what the assay is, and is not, designed to detect.

The most important scientific discipline in this exercise is restraint. A
sequence-perfect candidate is not a clinical test. A beautiful phylogenetic tree
does not prove transmission. A negative in-silico cross-reactivity search does
not replace wet-lab validation. For molecular diagnostics, evidence must be
layered: genomic rationale first, analytical performance second, clinical
performance third, and public-health utility last.

## Reproducibility Checklist

- [ ] Define intended target scope: Andes virus only, outbreak lineage, or broad
      hantavirus screen.
- [ ] Freeze accession lists and database versions.
- [ ] Curate sequences by segment, quality, geography, host, and date.
- [ ] Align S, M, and L segments separately.
- [ ] Build segment-specific phylogenies.
- [ ] Identify conserved target windows.
- [ ] Rank primer/probe candidates without publishing operational sequences.
- [ ] Run in-silico inclusivity against target diversity.
- [ ] Run in-silico exclusivity against near neighbors and background organisms.
- [ ] Document limitations and candidate-selection rationale.
- [ ] Define the laboratory validation plan without claiming validation.
- [ ] Add periodic sequence-surveillance triggers.

## Sources

- CDC. [Andes Virus Outbreak on a Cruise Ship: Current Situation](https://www.cdc.gov/hantavirus/situation-summary/).
  Accessed 22 May 2026.
- CDC Health Alert Network. [2026 Multi-country Hantavirus Cluster Linked to
  Cruise Ship](https://www.cdc.gov/han/php/notices/han00528.html). 8 May 2026.
- ECDC. [Andes hantavirus outbreak in cruise ship, 21 May
  2026](https://www.ecdc.europa.eu/en/infectious-disease-topics/hantavirus-infection/surveillance-and-updates/andes-hantavirus-outbreak).
- WHO. [Hantavirus cluster linked to cruise ship travel, Multi-country](https://www.who.int/emergencies/disease-outbreak-news/item/2026-DON601).
  Disease Outbreak News, 13 May 2026.
- Virological. [Preliminary analysis of *Orthohantavirus andesense* virus
  sequences from a cruise-ship related cluster, May
  2026](https://virological.org/t/preliminary-analysis-of-orthohantavirus-andesense-virus-sequences-from-a-cruise-ship-related-cluster-may-2026/1029).
- ICTV. [Genus *Orthohantavirus*](https://ictv.global/report/chapter/hantaviridae/hantaviridae/mammantavirinae/orthohantavirus).
- NCBI. [Design PCR primers and check them for
  specificity](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/guide/howto/design-pcr-primers/).
- CLSI. [MM03: Molecular Diagnostic Methods for Infectious
  Diseases](https://clsi.org/shop/standards/mm03/).
- CLSI. [MM17: Validation and Verification of Multiplex Nucleic Acid
  Assays](https://clsi.org/shop/standards/mm17/).
- ATCC. [Infectious Disease Assay Development: Establishing Inclusivity
  Exclusivity](https://www.atcc.org/resources/white-papers/infectious-disease-assay-development-establishing-inclusivity-exclusivity).
